title: Employee Rights and Privileges: Immunity
description: School district employees are protected from personal legal liability when they act in good faith while carrying out their official job duties. This protection encourages staff to make decisions and take actions in the best interest of students without fear of personal lawsuits. Understanding these protections helps the community know how accountability is structured when questions arise about employee conduct.

timeline_local.summary: This is a new local policy (DGC, Version 1) for Conroe ISD that establishes definitions and immunity protections for professional employees from disciplinary proceedings when they report Chapter 37 violations or take good-faith actions to remove disruptive, unruly, or bullying students from class. The entire policy is new language, driven by a legislative change.
timeline_local.changes_added: ["Definitions — For this Policy, "disciplinary proceeding" means: 1. An action brought by the school district employing a professional employee of a school district to discharge or suspend the employee or terminate or not renew the employee's term contract; or 2. An action or proceeding brought by the State Board for Educator Certification.", "Immunity from Disciplinary Proceedings — A professional employee of a school district may not be subject to disciplinary proceedings for: 1. The reporting of a violation of Chapter 37 under the Texas Education Code to another professional employee of a school district, the agency, or a law enforcement agency; or 2. An action taken in good faith to remove a student from class consistent with Texas Education Code § 37.002, including removal of a student who: a. Repeatedly interferes with the teacher's ability to communicate effectively with the students in the class or with the ability of the student's classmates to learn; or b. Demonstrates behavior that is unruly, disruptive, or abusive toward the teacher, another adult, or another student; or c. Engages in conduct that constitutes bullying.", "The immunity provided by this policy is in addition to any other immunity provided by law and may not be construed to interfere with any other immunity provided by law."]
timeline_local.notes: This appears to be a brand-new policy (Version 1) rather than a revision to existing text — all content is shown in red underline, indicating entirely new language with nothing removed. The cover page does not contain a large bold action-determining heading (e.g., "Discuss," "Adopt," etc.); it only shows metadata fields, so the board action could not be determined from the title heading convention and is marked 'unknown'. The rationale is listed as a legislative change.

elements.element: Definition of 'Disciplinary Proceeding' (district discharge/suspension/contract termination or nonrenewal; SBEC action)
elements.statutory_basis: TEC § 22.05121(a), enacted by H.B. 6, 89th Leg., R.S., Ch. 1066, eff. June 20, 2025
elements.supporting_quote: A professional employee of a school district may not be subject to disciplinary proceedings for: (1) the reporting of a violation of Chapter 37 to another professional employee of a school district, the agency, or a law enforcement agency; or (2) an action taken in good faith to remove a student from class under Section 37.002.
elements.verification_method: web search confirmed — TEC § 22.05121 text confirmed at statutes.capitol.texas.gov
elements.local_additions: The policy's two-part numbered definition of 'disciplinary proceeding' mirrors the statutory definition verbatim. No locally added categories are present.

elements.element: Immunity from disciplinary proceedings for reporting a Chapter 37 violation to another professional employee, the agency, or a law enforcement agency
elements.statutory_basis: TEC § 22.05121(b)(1), enacted by H.B. 6, 89th Leg., R.S., Ch. 1066, eff. June 20, 2025
elements.supporting_quote: A professional employee of a school district may not be subject to disciplinary proceedings for: (1) the reporting of a violation of Chapter 37 to another professional employee of a school district, the agency, or a law enforcement agency
elements.verification_method: web search confirmed — verbatim statutory text confirmed at statutes.capitol.texas.gov
elements.local_additions: None. Policy language tracks the statute verbatim. The mandatory 'may not be subject to' language compels the district to recognize and implement this immunity in its own proceedings.

elements.element: Immunity from disciplinary proceedings for good-faith removal of a student who repeatedly interferes with the teacher's ability to communicate effectively or with classmates' ability to learn (TEC § 37.002(b)(1))
elements.statutory_basis: TEC § 22.05121(b)(2) and TEC § 37.002(b)(1), as amended by H.B. 6, 89th Leg., R.S., Ch. 1066, eff. June 20, 2025
elements.supporting_quote: A teacher may remove from class a student: (1) who has been documented by the teacher to repeatedly interfere with the teacher's ability to communicate effectively with the students in the class or with the ability of the student's classmates to learn
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text — confirmed in statute analysis
elements.local_additions: None. The sub-category tracks TEC § 37.002(b)(1) removal grounds directly.

elements.element: Immunity from disciplinary proceedings for good-faith removal of a student who demonstrates unruly, disruptive, or abusive behavior toward the teacher, another adult, or another student (TEC § 37.002(b)(2))
elements.statutory_basis: TEC § 22.05121(b)(2) and TEC § 37.002(b)(2), as amended by H.B. 6, 89th Leg., R.S., Ch. 1066, eff. June 20, 2025
elements.supporting_quote: whose behavior the teacher determines is so unruly, disruptive, or abusive that it seriously interferes with the teacher's ability to communicate effectively with the students in the class or with the ability of the student's classmates to learn
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text — confirmed in statute analysis
elements.local_additions: The statute requires that unruly, disruptive, or abusive behavior 'seriously interferes' with teaching or learning. The policy omits the 'seriously interferes' threshold qualifier and instead states the behavior is 'unruly, disruptive, or abusive toward the teacher, another adult, or another student.' This paraphrase is a locally chosen drafting adaptation that broadens the immunity's articulated scope beyond the statutory standard, making this element Mixed.

elements.element: Immunity from disciplinary proceedings for good-faith removal of a student who engages in bullying as defined by TEC § 37.0832 (TEC § 37.002(b)(3))
elements.statutory_basis: TEC § 22.05121(b)(2) and TEC § 37.002(b)(3), as amended by H.B. 6, 89th Leg., R.S., Ch. 1066, eff. June 20, 2025
elements.supporting_quote: who engages in conduct that constitutes bullying, as defined by Section 37.0832.
elements.verification_method: verbatim statute text — confirmed in statute analysis
elements.local_additions: None. Policy language mirrors the statutory removal ground verbatim.

elements.element: Statement that immunity under this policy is cumulative and may not be construed to interfere with any other immunity provided by law
elements.statutory_basis: TEC § 22.05121(c), enacted by H.B. 6, 89th Leg., R.S., Ch. 1066, eff. June 20, 2025
elements.supporting_quote: The immunity provided by Subsection (b) is in addition to any other immunity provided by law. This section may not be construed to interfere with any other immunity provided by law.
elements.verification_method: web search confirmed — verbatim text confirmed at statutes.capitol.texas.gov
elements.local_additions: The policy substitutes 'provided by this policy' for 'provided by Subsection (b),' which is an immaterial local drafting adaptation for readability. No substantive local additions are present.

compliance_summary: New Immunity Policy: Texas H.B. 6 (89th Legislature), effective June 20, 2025, required school districts to create protections for teachers facing disciplinary action in certain situations. Conroe ISD's new policy DGC, Version 1 puts those required protections into writing for the first time.

Reporting Protection: Under TEC § 22.05121(b)(1), teachers cannot be disciplined for reporting a Chapter 37 violation to another staff member, the Texas Education Agency, or a law enforcement agency. The policy reflects this requirement using language that closely follows the state statute.

Student Removal Protection: TEC § 22.05121(b)(2) prohibits the district from disciplining a teacher who removes a student from class in good faith under the grounds listed in TEC § 37.002(b). The policy covers three removal situations: a student who repeatedly disrupts learning, a student who behaves in an unruly or abusive way, or a student who engages in bullying as defined by TEC § 37.0832.

Behavior Wording Difference: For the second removal situation involving unruly or abusive behavior, TEC § 37.002(b)(2) requires that the behavior 'seriously interferes' with teaching or learning before a teacher can remove a student. The policy as written describes the behavior but leaves out that 'seriously interferes' threshold, which means the policy's wording is broader than what the statute says.

Other Legal Protections Preserved: TEC § 22.05121(c) requires that the new immunity not be read to cancel out any other legal protections a teacher may already have. The policy includes this requirement, noting that its protections are meant to add to, not replace, any other existing legal protections.

timeline_local.summary: This is a new local policy (DGC, Version 1) for Conroe ISD establishing employee immunity from disciplinary proceedings. It defines "disciplinary proceeding" and protects professional employees from discipline for reporting Chapter 37 violations or taking good-faith actions to remove disruptive, unruly, or bullying students from class.
changes_added: Definitions section: For the purpose of this policy, "disciplinary proceeding" means: (1) An action brought by the school district employing a professional employee of a school district to discharge or suspend the employee or terminate or not renew the employee's term contract; or (2) An action or proceeding brought by the State Board for Educator Certification.

Immunity from Disciplinary Proceedings section: A professional employee of a school district may not be subject to disciplinary proceedings for: (1) The reporting of a violation of Chapter 37 under Texas Education Code to another professional employee of a school district, the agency, or a law enforcement agency; or (2) An action taken in good faith to remove a student from class: a. Who repeatedly interferes with the teacher's ability to communicate effectively with the students in the class or with the ability of the student's classmates to learn; or b. Who demonstrates behavior that is unruly, disruptive, or abusive toward the teacher, another adult, or another student; or c. Engages in conduct that constitutes bullying.
timeline_local.notes: The entire document appears to be new (Version 1, all text in red underline), as there is no prior policy language shown. The cover page title heading is the large bold text "Employee Rights And Privileges: Immunity (DGC)" — this does not match any of the standard action title patterns (e.g., "Discuss Revisions," "Adopt Revisions," etc.), so the action has been set to "unknown." The board action cannot be definitively determined from the provided cover page. There are no red strikethrough removals since this is a first-version policy creation.

description_local: This policy document covers updates to five separate school district policies affecting both employees and students, including staff legal protections, student grading and progress reporting, medical treatment procedures, crisis intervention protocols, and child abuse and neglect reporting. The changes affect teachers, other school employees, students, and their families, and were made to bring district practices in line with recent Texas state legislation. Parents and community members should be aware of these updates because they directly impact how the school communicates student progress, responds to health and safety emergencies, and fulfills its legal obligations to protect children from abuse and neglect.
